quote: I know. I do. You know, I actually thought this was probably through the state for some reason. You know, they had that program going where you can get free solar for all. Mhmm. Have you heard about it?
stewart_read: Sanobio's state-program expectation is the call's central misalignment. He filled out a form believing he might access a free Illinois solar program; Ion is a private installer. Ella's response at 03:01 acknowledges 'various state incentives' but doesn't validate the specific program Sanobio named, doesn't close the gap cleanly, and doesn't bridge to why Ion's program is worth considering alongside or instead of waiting. This is a state_program_expectation_gap — a TBD rebuttal in the codex — and the closest available tools are rebuttals.changed_mind (curiosity creation) and coaching_philosophy.objection_inversion (flip the wait cost). Neither was deployed. Note: this rebuttal gap should be flagged for Kenny to populate in the codex.
alternative_hypothesis: Strategic read: Ella may have intentionally avoided engaging the state-program claim to prevent a debate she couldn't win, choosing instead to keep moving toward the estimate. Rejected — there's no evidence of a later setup move, and the expectation gap resurfaces implicitly in Sanobio's 'catch 22' framing at 08:06.
coaching_implication: Pull this moment in the 1-on-1. Ask Ella: 'What did you know about the Illinois free solar program, and what would you say to a customer who's waiting for it?' The correct move is to validate the program's existence, then bridge: the cost of waiting is real regardless of whether the program materializes on Sanobio's timeline. A suggested rephrase (intent, not verbatim): 'That program is real — and if it comes through for you, great. But those programs have timelines that aren't in your control. What we can do is show you what your bill looks like if you don't wait. If the numbers don't work, you walk away with information.' This is an objection_inversion opportunity the floor is missing universally per the codex.

quote: Well, I'm not gonna do it. I won't do it. Because I heard, you know, I heard about they have some that's out here with the state of Illinois. We're doing some for free. So I was just curious. There's nothing I'm getting ready to spend money on or waste your time. I'll wait until I'm ready.
stewart_read: This is the call's exit moment. Sanobio is polite but explicit: he's not spending money, he's not wasting Ella's time, he'll wait. Ella's response at 03:46 is a process-reframe ('we can definitely still just get you out that estimate') with a thin state-incentives acknowledgment. She doesn't validate Sanobio's reasoning, doesn't acknowledge the cost of waiting, and doesn't create curiosity about what the numbers might show. The empathy miss here is subtle — Sanobio isn't disclosing vulnerability, but he is disclosing a real decision framework (waiting for free > paying now). Ella skips past the framework entirely.
alternative_hypothesis: Strategic read: Ella may have judged that engaging the state-program debate would lose the call faster than pivoting to process. Partially supported — the pivot did keep Sanobio on the line. But 'kept him on the line' and 'converted his posture' are different outcomes, and only the latter produces a durable sit.
coaching_implication: This is the flip opportunity. The codex's objection_inversion pattern applies directly: 'What you can't afford is your current bill.' Ella had Sanobio's bill pain on record ($150-$250/month, spiked to $250). A suggested rephrase (intent, not verbatim): 'I totally get that — and if that program comes through, that's great. But while you're waiting, ComEd is still charging you. The estimate just shows you what that waiting is costing. You don't have to do anything with it.' No fabricated numbers needed — the emotional logic is the leverage.

quote: And then it would be easier if you sent me a photo of that through email or through text message.
stewart_read: Ella asks for a photo before confirming the channel. Sanobio's response at 06:21 — 'What? Text message at this number? This is it?' — reveals he's on a landline. Ella then pivots to texting from her own number, which is the correct recovery, but the sequence is backwards: the landline_protocol in the codex requires confirming the channel before requesting a photo. The error cost roughly 45 seconds of confusion and required Ella to send a text from her personal line — the anti-pattern the codex flags explicitly (bill_collection.stewart_automated_bill_capture.detection_today).
coaching_implication: Quick fix: before requesting any bill photo, confirm 'Are you on a cell phone or a landline?' One question eliminates the channel confusion entirely. Also flag: Ella gave out her personal number for bill follow-up (implicit in 'I did just send you a text message' at 06:51). Per the codex's detection_today note, this is a privacy/scale exposure that should be flagged for the floor's bill-capture workflow review.

quote: Yeah. That's a catch 22 there. So that means once you guys see what the bill is, then you guys can charge what you want.
stewart_read: Sanobio has reverse-engineered a pricing trap: if Ion prices just below his current bill, the savings are nominal and Ion captures the margin. This is a sophisticated objection — not a scam concern, but a structural critique of the value proposition. Ella's response ('it has to be lower than that electricity bill') is technically accurate but doesn't address the 'penny lower' scenario Sanobio names at 08:20. The bill was captured but never emotionally flipped — Ella never used Sanobio's own bill pain ($150-$250, spiked to $250) as the anchor for why the savings are real and meaningful, not marginal.
alternative_hypothesis: Strategic read: Ella may have judged that engaging the pricing mechanics further would create more objections than it resolved, choosing instead to redirect to the estimate. Partially supported — she does redirect correctly. But the bill was never flipped, which means the closer inherits this pricing anxiety without any emotional groundwork laid.
coaching_implication: This is the bill_anchoring failure the codex identifies as universal across the corpus. Ella captured the bill ($150-$250) but dropped it. The flip was available: 'You're already paying $150-$250 a month to ComEd — that's the number we're replacing, not adding to. The specialist shows you the exact comparison.' No fabricated numbers — Sanobio's own stated bill is the anchor. Pull this in the 1-on-1 and walk through the capture-and-flip sequence.

quote: Okay. And approximately how much per kilowatt hour do you charge?
stewart_read: Sanobio asks a direct technical pricing question. Ella's deflection to the specialist is correct scope discipline — this is closer-tier content. However, the deflection at 09:54 is bare: 'that's just something that my specialist knows all about.' There's no bridge to why the specialist conversation is worth Sanobio's time, no tie-back to his bill pain, and no curiosity creation. The codex's universal_escape_hatch ('That's exactly the kind of question our specialist is built to answer') is present in spirit but thin in execution.
coaching_implication: The scope discipline is correct — don't coach Ella away from the deflection. Coach the bridge. A suggested rephrase (intent, not verbatim): 'That's exactly what the specialist walks you through — they'll show you the per-kWh rate against what ComEd is charging you right now, so you can see the actual comparison.' The bridge keeps scope discipline AND creates curiosity. Thirty seconds of additional framing here materially reduces the closer's cold-start problem.

quote: If you can just do a phone call, that'll be fine. What number would they be calling me from? Because I have a screening service on my phone number, and it usually blocks numbers. See, that's why your number didn't really comes through. I happen to, you know, see it where it showed a missed call.
stewart_read: Sanobio surfaces a concrete logistical barrier to the appointment being held: his call-screening service blocks unknown numbers. Ella's response at 10:39 — 'before the appointment, we can definitely send you over their number' — is a reasonable promise, but it's a promise the closer must fulfill, not Ella. This is a conditional booking: the sit depends on the closer's number being sent in advance and Sanobio recognizing it. The button-up that follows is also incomplete — no 'is that fair' handshake, no computer/no-driving anchor, no one-hour block stated per button_up.required_elements.
coaching_implication: Two things for the manager to address: (1) The call-screening promise must be in the handoff brief — the closer needs to send their number before 10:30AM tomorrow or the call won't connect. (2) Walk Ella through the full button_up checklist. The 'is that fair' handshake and the computer/no-driving anchor are both missing here. On a fragile sit like this one, skipping the verbal handshake is a meaningful no-show risk multiplier.

stewart_read: Sanobio's 'too much' is an emotional signal, not just a data point. He follows it with a specific spike ($250) and a frustration marker ('it's crazy right now'). Ella's response at 02:09 — 'I've just seen that happen lately. Like, I don't know what's up with this year, but those bills have just been going up and up' — is a rapport-building acknowledgment, but it's the last time the bill pain is used as emotional leverage. The bill is captured and then dropped. Per coaching_philosophy.bill_anchoring, the failure pattern is 'ask + acknowledge + move on' — which is exactly what happens here.
coaching_implication: This is the flip opportunity the codex identifies as universally missed. Ella had the emotional signal ('too much,' '$250 spike') and the rapport moment ('I've seen that happen'). The next line should have been the flip: 'That's exactly why we're calling — ComEd keeps going up, and the program we have locks in a lower rate. Let's see what that looks like for your home.' Instead, Ella moves directly to the credit qualifier. Pull this sequence in the 1-on-1 as the canonical bill-anchor-and-flip example.
